title: Digestion breaks food into absorbable nutrients
summary: The digestive system breaks carbohydrates into simple sugars, proteins into amino acids, and fats into fatty acids before absorption.
bodyMarkdown: The digestive system breaks carbohydrates into simple sugars, proteins into amino acids, and fats into fatty acids and glycerol before absorption. Nerves and hormones regulate the process. **App context:** This is why we explain what the body does with meals, not just log numbers.

title: Body weight is an energy-balance problem
summary: NIDDK describes body weight regulation as influenced by appetite, satiety, body composition, sedentary behaviour, and physical activity.
bodyMarkdown: NIDDK's Body Weight Planner models dynamic changes rather than the simplistic '3,500 kcal per pound' rule. As calories change, the body responds in ways that offset the deficit over time.

title: Higher protein helps preserve lean mass during energy deficit
summary: During energy deficit, protein intake above the RDA helps preserve lean body mass, especially when paired with exercise.
bodyMarkdown: Protein targets should be personalised by age, activity, and energy deficit with stronger emphasis for older adults and users trying to lose fat while preserving function.

title: Fiber supports digestion, fullness, and heart health
summary: Dietary fiber slows digestion, supports gut health, helps manage blood sugar, and can reduce cholesterol.
bodyMarkdown: WHO recommends fibre-rich carbohydrates. South African staples like samp, beans, whole grains, and vegetables are natural fibre sources. Aim for 25-35g per day.

title: Too much sodium can raise blood pressure
summary: WHO recommends sodium intake below 2 g/day (salt below 5 g/day). Most dietary sodium comes from packaged and restaurant foods.
bodyMarkdown: South Africa introduced a Health Promotion Levy on sugary drinks and mandatory fortification of maize meal and wheat flour. Reducing sodium and increasing potassium helps blood pressure.

title: Potassium helps balance sodium and supports blood pressure
summary: WHO suggests potassium intake from food of at least 3,510 mg/day. Sources include beans, spinach, potatoes, bananas, and morogo.
bodyMarkdown: Potassium-rich foods help offset sodium's blood-pressure effects. South African sources include morogo (wild spinach), beans, potatoes, bananas, and samp.

title: Ultra-processed foods can drive overeating
summary: In controlled trials, adults eating an ultra-processed diet consumed about 500 extra kcal per day compared with a minimally processed diet.
bodyMarkdown: This finding from an NIH inpatient RCT shows that food quality and meal structure matter beyond simple calorie counting.

title: Short sleep can increase hunger
summary: Sleeping less than 7 hours can affect appetite regulation and make late-night eating more likely.
bodyMarkdown: Short sleep has been linked with lower leptin and higher ghrelin, which may increase appetite. NHLBI recommends 7-9 hours for most adults.

title: Stress can increase hunger signals
summary: Stress-level glucocorticoids can increase hunger and metabolic signals related to eating. Chronic stress is linked with weight gain patterns in some groups.
bodyMarkDown: WHO's stress guide supports practical daily coping skills. App context: offer stress-aware coaching rather than moralising when adherence drops.

title: Hydration supports energy, digestion, and recovery
summary: DRI-based water guidance varies by body size, activity, and climate. Dehydration beyond 2% body mass can impair performance.
bodyMarkDown: Default water target: 35ml per kg body weight. Adjust for hot South African weather, exercise, illness, or pregnancy. Do not gamify extreme fluid intake.

title: Waist circumference adds risk information beyond weight
summary: Abdominal fat distribution changes cardiometabolic risk. Waist circumference can add risk information when BMI alone is incomplete.
bodyMarkDown: Me Body prioritises trend weight, waist, and function over daily scale noise or aesthetic framing.

title: Daily weight can fluctuate by 0.5-2 kg for non-fat reasons
summary: Daily weight changes are often water, sodium, carbs, digestion, or training stress, not fat gain or loss.
bodyMarkDown: Track the trend over 7-14 days, not the daily number. One high reading does not mean the plan failed.

title: More daily steps are linked to lower mortality risk
summary: Evidence shows an inverse dose-response relationship between daily steps and all-cause and cardiovascular mortality, with benefits starting well below 10,000 steps.
bodyMarkDown: Personalize step goals progressively. Even 4,000-6,000 steps show benefit.

title: Resistance training supports muscle, bone, and metabolism
summary: ACSM's 2026 update emphasises that consistency matters more than complicated programmes. Home-based methods work.
bodyMarkDown: WHO recommends muscle-strengthening activities on 2+ days per week. Even bodyweight exercises count.

title: Menstrual cycle response is highly individual
summary: Evidence does not support a universal phase-based training prescription. Symptoms are common and meaningful but individual.
bodyMarkDown: Me Body is cycle-aware, not cycle-deterministic. Track symptoms, allow flexible targets, and explain that individual response is key.

title: After 60, lean mass tends to decrease and fat mass increases
summary: Resistance training plus adequate protein supports strength and function more reliably than muscle mass changes alone.
bodyMarkDown: Older adults should combine aerobic, strengthening, and balance activities. WHO defines healthy ageing in terms of functional ability.

title: Exercise in uncomplicated pregnancy is recommended
summary: ACOG recommends at least 150 minutes of moderate activity per week in uncomplicated pregnancy.
bodyMarkDown: Stop and seek help for chest pain, dizziness, severe shortness of breath, vaginal bleeding, or contractions.

title: Some medicines can change appetite, fluid balance, or weight trend
summary: Insulin, sulfonylureas, some antipsychotics, several antidepressants, glucocorticoids, and beta-blockers may affect weight.
bodyMarkDown: This is a body response, not a failure. Me Body adapts the plan around medications. Never stop prescribed medication without consulting your doctor.

title: The body adapts to calorie deficits
summary: As calories change, the body responds in ways that offset the deficit over time. Adaptive thermogenesis exists.
bodyMarkDown: This is why Me Body adjusts targets from trend data, not static prescriptions. You are not failing — your body is adapting.

title: Calorie-hidden mode is available for gentler tracking
summary: Some people prefer to step away from numbers for a healthier relationship with food and body.
bodyMarkDown: In calorie-hidden mode, Me Body focuses on meal balance, food quality, habits, and how you feel rather than exact calorie and macro numbers.

title: South Africa is experiencing a nutrition transition
summary: SA research describes a shift toward more processed and packaged foods and away from vegetables, alongside high sugar-sweetened beverage intake.
bodyMarkDown: SADHS 2016 found that 36% of adults consumed sugar-sweetened beverages the day before, averaging 607ml. Local portion guides and mixed-dish modelling are essential.

title: South Africa has the highest adult obesity rate in the African Region
summary: WHO regional data reports about 3 in 10 SA adults were obese in 2022. SADHS 2016 found 68% of women and 31% of men overweight or obese.
bodyMarkDown: This makes local, culturally relevant, non-stigmatising support especially important. Me Body includes SA meal templates, local food names, and household portion guides.

title: WHO recommends carbohydrate quality over restriction
summary: WHO recommends carbohydrates at 45-75% of energy, emphasizing minimally processed, fibre-rich sources.
bodyMarkDown: Free sugars should be under 10% of energy, with further reduction below 5% suggested. SA staples like pap, brown bread, samp, and sweet potato are quality carbohydrate sources.

title: WHO recommends total fat at 30% or less of energy
summary: Saturated fat should be under 10% of energy and trans fat under 1%.
bodyMarkDown: Fat quality matters. Choose vegetable oils, nuts, seeds, avocado, and fatty fish over solid fats and processed oils.

title: Mifflin-St Jeor equation estimates resting energy needs
summary: The Mifflin-St Jeor equation uses weight, height, age, and sex to estimate basal metabolic rate, then multiplies by activity level for TDEE.
bodyMarkDown: Male: 10w + 6.25h - 5a + 5. Female: 10w + 6.25h - 5a - 161. These are starting estimates — adjust from trend data.

title: Me Body is not medical advice
summary: Me Body provides general wellness and nutrition tracking tools. It is not medical diagnosis or treatment.
bodyMarkDown: Speak to a qualified healthcare professional before making major diet, exercise, or health changes, especially with medical conditions, pregnancy, eating disorder history, or medications.
